did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a two-stage ensemble deep learning framework for skin cancer diagnosis using dermoscopic images. It integrates three deep learning models (MobileNetV2, InceptionV3, and EfficientNetV2B3) with a soft voting strategy to classify lesions as benign, non-melanoma, or melanoma. The models were trained and evaluated on the HAM10000 and PH2 datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving a proposed two-stage ensemble deep learning framework for skin cancer diagnosis. It details the methodology (integrating MobileNetV2, InceptionV3, and EfficientNetV2B3), describes the datasets used (HAM10000 and PH2), and reports specific performance results (75% overall accuracy, 86% for EfficientNetV2B3). Although it includes a literature survey section, the primary contribution is the development and validation of a new technical system, classifying it as a research paper.

title: Two-Stage Ensemble Learning for Skin Cancer Diagnosis Using Deep Learning Approaches
journal: 2025 5th International Conference on Emerging Research in Electronics, Computer Science and Technology (ICERECT)
brief_summary: This study presents a two-stage ensemble deep learning framework for skin cancer diagnosis using dermoscopic images to address challenges like class imbalance and false negatives. The system integrates MobileNetV2, InceptionV3, and EfficientNetV2B3 via a soft voting strategy, initially classifying lesions as benign, non-melanoma, or melanoma, followed by a secondary binary classification for benign cases to reduce diagnostic oversight. The models were trained and evaluated on the HAM10000 and PH2 datasets using an 80:20 train-test split and 5-fold cross-validation. The ensemble achieved an overall classification accuracy of 75%, while EfficientNetV2B3 individually attained the highest accuracy of 86%. The methodology demonstrates improved diagnostic reliability and is positioned as suitable for real-time clinical use and telemedicine applications.

lesion_types_studied.details: Classified as malignant cancer in the HAM10000 dataset (Class ID 1). Used in the primary multi-class classification stage and the overall skin cancer diagnosis task.
Classified as malignant in the HAM10000 dataset (Class ID 2). Included in the primary multi-class classification stage.
Classified as non-cancerous in the HAM10000 dataset (Class ID 3). Included in the primary multi-class classification stage.
Classified as precancerous in the HAM10000 dataset (Class ID 4). Included in the primary multi-class classification stage.
Classified as non-cancerous in the HAM10000 dataset (Class ID 5). Included in the primary multi-class classification stage.
Classified as non-cancerous in the HAM10000 dataset (Class ID 6). Included in the primary multi-class classification stage.

A high-level category used in the two-stage ensemble framework's primary classifier. Lesions classified as benign in the first stage undergo secondary binary classification.
A high-level category used in the two-stage ensemble framework's primary classifier, distinct from melanoma and benign.

all_task_types.target: Lesion classes (benign, non-melanoma, melanoma) and binary malignancy risk for benign cases.
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of skin lesions into three categories (benign, non-melanoma, melanoma) using a two-stage ensemble deep learning framework. A secondary binary classification is applied to lesions initially classified as benign to reassess malignancy risk. The task involves predicting discrete diagnostic categories using dermoscopic images from the HAM10000 and PH2 datasets.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, developed by Tschandl et al. It includes images from different populations and studies.

use.training: Used for training the deep learning models (MobileNetV2, InceptionV3, EfficientNetV2B3). The dataset is described as big and diverse to train large robust models.

datasets_used.name: PH2
datasets_used.description: A dermoscopic image database for research and benchmarking, containing high-resolution professional annotated images.

use.testing: Used for testing and adjusting the light/inference of the ensemble model. It is described as high-quality and used for testing.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used to validate the model performance during training and testing.
Used to divide the datasets (HAM10000 and PH2) into training and testing subsets.
Used to aggregate probabilistic outputs from multiple models (MobileNetV2, InceptionV3, EfficientNetV2B3) to make final predictions, reducing bias and improving generalization.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and false negative rate.

HAM10000.metric: Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-score, False Negative Rate (FNR)
HAM10000.notes: EfficientNetV2B3 demonstrated the highest standalone accuracy of 86% (reported as 86.1% in Table). The ensemble achieved an overall classification accuracy of 75%. The system showed consistent performance across datasets with no significant drop in accuracy for images representing darker skin tones.

PH2.notes: The PH2 dataset (200 high-resolution images) was used for testing and adjusting the light/inference of the ensemble model. The system's performance was consistent across both datasets (HAM10000 and PH2).

architecture_used.main_model: Two-Stage Ensemble Learning Framework integrating MobileNetV2, InceptionV3, and EfficientNetV2B3
architecture_used.pretraining: Pretrained on ImageNet; base layers frozen, only final layers fine-tuned

architecture_details.ensemble_strategy: Soft voting ensemble strategy aggregating probabilistic outputs

two_stage_process.stage_2: Binary validation for lesions classified as benign in stage 1

data_preprocessing: Color normalization (pixel values and luminance to [0, 1], zero-mean normalization)
Data augmentation (random rotations, flips, scaling, brightness adjustments)
Class rebalancing (oversampling minority classes with class weights)
training_details.transfer_learning: Fine-tuning only final layers; base layers frozen

classification_or_output_head.details: Retrained on dermoscopic data; soft voting aggregates probabilities from individual model heads

preprocessing_techniques: Image resizing to 224x224 pixels using bicubic interpolation
Color normalization: pixel values and luminance normalized to [0, 1] followed by zero-mean normalization
Data augmentation: random rotations, flips, scaling, and brightness adjustments
Class rebalancing: oversampling minority classes (melanoma and others) with class weights to enhance recall

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Random rotations, flips, and scaling were applied to the training images to avoid overfitting and address class imbalance.
Brightness adjustments were applied as part of the augmentation process.
Minority classes (specifically melanoma and others) were up-sampled using class weights to enhance recall.

conclusions: The proposed two-stage ensemble learning framework, integrating MobileNetV2, InceptionV3, and EfficientNetV2B3 via soft voting, achieved an overall classification accuracy of 75% on the HAM10000 dataset.
Among individual models, EfficientNetV2B3 demonstrated the highest standalone accuracy of 86.1% (reported as 86% in the abstract), particularly excelling in melanoma detection.

The two-stage approach, which applies a secondary binary classification to lesions initially identified as benign, successfully reduced the false negative rate to 6.3%, addressing the critical clinical need to minimize missed melanoma diagnoses.

The system demonstrated consistent performance across the HAM10000 and PH2 datasets, with no significant drop in accuracy for images representing darker skin tones, indicating improved fairness and generalizability across diverse patient demographics.

MobileNetV2 exhibited superior computational efficiency, making it suitable for real-time applications and deployment in low-resource or remote settings, while InceptionV3 provided balanced performance across lesion types.
The methodology is positioned as well-suited for real-time clinical use, telemedicine platforms, and mobile health applications due to its balance of accuracy, interpretability, and efficiency.

The study acknowledges that while the model addresses class imbalance and demographic bias, further enhancement through explainable AI and large-scale validation is necessary for broader clinical adoption.
important_remarks: The paper is a conference publication (ICERECT 2025) with limited scope for extensive clinical validation compared to journal articles.

The ensemble model achieved an overall accuracy of 75%, which is lower than the best individual model (EfficientNetV2B3 at 86.1%), suggesting potential information loss or suboptimal fusion in the ensemble strategy.
The PH2 dataset (200 images) was used for testing/inference rather than training, limiting the diversity of the evaluation set and potentially inflating performance consistency claims.

The claim of 'no significant drop in accuracy for images representing darker skin tones' is not supported by demographic breakdowns or statistical tests in the provided context; this is a strong assertion requiring rigorous validation.
The paper does not report hyperparameters such as learning rate, batch size, or number of epochs, limiting reproducibility.
The two-stage approach reduces false negatives to 6.3% but may introduce complexity and latency, potentially impacting real-time deployment efficiency despite the use of MobileNetV2.
No external validation on independent, multi-center datasets was performed; reliance on HAM10000 and PH2 limits generalizability to other clinical populations.
The study acknowledges the need for explainable AI and large-scale validation for broader clinical adoption, indicating current limitations in interpretability and robustness.
